The Eisenhower administration had created a plan to overthrow [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro")'s regime though an invasion of Cuba by a counter-revolutionary insurgency composed of U.S.-trained, anti-Castro [Cuban exiles](/wiki/Cuban_exile "Cuban exile")[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2002233,_238-199)[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGleijeses1995-200) led by [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") paramilitary officers.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199369–73-201) Kennedy had campaigned on a hardline stance against Castro, and when presented with the plan that had been developed under the Eisenhower administration, he enthusiastically adopted it regardless of the risk of inflaming tensions with the Soviet Union.[[200]](#cite_note-fiftyyearslater-202) Kennedy approved the final invasion plan on April 4, 1961.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

On April 15, 1961, eight CIA-supplied [B-26](/wiki/Douglas_A-26_Invader "Douglas A-26 Invader") bombers left Nicaragua to bomb Cuban airfields. The bombers missed many of their targets, leaving most of Castro's air force intact.[[202]](#cite_note-204) On April 17, the 1,500 U.S.-trained Cuban exile invasion force, known as [Brigade 2506](/wiki/Brigade_2506 "Brigade 2506"), landed at beaches along the [Bay of Pigs](/wiki/Bay_of_Pigs "Bay of Pigs") and immediately came under heavy fire.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199371,_673-205) The goal was to spark a widespread popular uprising against Castro, but no such uprising occurred.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrinkley201268–69-206) No U.S. air support was provided.[[205]](#cite_note-207) The invading force was defeated within two days by the [Cuban Revolutionary Armed Forces](/wiki/Cuban_Revolutionary_Armed_Forces "Cuban Revolutionary Armed Forces");[[206]](#cite_note-208) 114 were killed and Kennedy was forced to negotiate for the release of the 1,189 survivors.[[207]](#cite_note-209) After twenty months, Cuba released the captured exiles in exchange for a ransom of $53 million worth of food and medicine.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2002268–294,_838–839-210) The incident made Castro wary of the U.S. and led him to believe that another invasion would take place.[[209]](#cite_note-211)

Biographer [Richard Reeves](/wiki/Richard_Reeves_(American_writer) "Richard Reeves (American writer)") said that Kennedy focused primarily on the political repercussions of the plan rather than military considerations. When it proved unsuccessful, he was convinced that the plan was a setup to make him look bad.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199395–97-212) He took responsibility for the failure, saying, "We got a big kick in the leg and we deserved it. But maybe we'll learn something from it."[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2002290,_295-213) Kennedy's approval ratings climbed afterwards, helped in part by the vocal support given to him by Nixon and Eisenhower.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003370–371-214) He appointed Robert Kennedy to help lead a committee to examine the causes of the failure.[[213]](#cite_note-215) The Kennedy administration [banned all Cuban imports](/wiki/United_States_embargo_against_Cuba "United States embargo against Cuba") and convinced the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States") (OAS) to expel Cuba.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008707–708-216)

#### Operation Mongoose

In late 1961, the White House formed the Special Group (Augmented), headed by Robert Kennedy and including [Edward Lansdale](/wiki/Edward_Lansdale "Edward Lansdale"), Secretary [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), and others. The group's objective—to overthrow Castro via espionage, sabotage, and other covert tactics—was never pursued.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993264-217) In November 1961, he authorized [Operation Mongoose](/wiki/Operation_Mongoose "Operation Mongoose").[[216]](#cite_note-eu.usatoday.com-218) In March 1962, Kennedy rejected [Operation Northwoods](/wiki/Operation_Northwoods "Operation Northwoods"), proposals for [false flag](/wiki/False_flag "False flag") attacks against American military and civilian targets,[[217]](#cite_note-1962USJCOS-219) and blaming them on the Cuban government to gain approval for a war against Cuba. However, the administration continued to plan for an invasion of Cuba in the summer of 1962.[[216]](#cite_note-eu.usatoday.com-218)

#### Cuban Missile Crisis

Main article: [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/President_Kennedy_-_signing_Cuba_Quarantine_Proclamation.jpg/250px-President_Kennedy_-_signing_Cuba_Quarantine_Proclamation.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Kennedy_-_signing_Cuba_Quarantine_Proclamation.jpg)

Kennedy signs the [Proclamation for Interdiction of the Delivery of Offensive Weapons to Cuba](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") in the Oval Office; c. October 23, 1962.

In the aftermath of the Bay of Pigs invasion, Khrushchev increased economic and military assistance to Cuba.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006203–205-220) The Soviet Union planned to allocate in Cuba 49 [medium-range ballistic missiles](/wiki/Medium-range_ballistic_missile "Medium-range ballistic missile"), 32 [intermediate-range ballistic missiles](/wiki/Intermediate-range_ballistic_missile "Intermediate-range ballistic missile"), 49 light [Il-28](/wiki/Ilyushin_Il-28 "Ilyushin Il-28") bombers and about 100 [tactical nuclear weapons](/wiki/Tactical_nuclear_weapon "Tactical nuclear weapon").[[219]](#cite_note-221) The Kennedy administration viewed the growing [Cuba-Soviet alliance](/wiki/Cuba%E2%80%93Soviet_Union_relations "Cuba–Soviet Union relations") with alarm, fearing that it could eventually pose a threat to the U.S.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrinkley2012113–114-222) On October 14, 1962, CIA [U-2](/wiki/Lockheed_U-2 "Lockheed U-2") spy planes [took photographs](/wiki/Aerial_reconnaissance#Cold_War "Aerial reconnaissance") of the Soviets' construction of intermediate-range ballistic missile sites in Cuba. The photos were shown to Kennedy on October 16; a consensus was reached that the missiles were offensive in nature and posed an immediate nuclear threat.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993345-223)

Kennedy faced a dilemma: if the U.S. attacked the sites, it might lead to nuclear war with the Soviet Union, but if the U.S. did nothing, it would be faced with the increased threat from close-range nuclear weapons (positioned approximately 90 mi (140 km) away from the Florida coast).[[222]](#cite_note-224) The U.S. would also appear to the world as less committed to the defense of the Western Hemisphere. On a personal level, Kennedy needed to show resolve in reaction to Khrushchev, especially after the Vienna summit.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993245-225) To deal with the crisis, he formed an ad-hoc body of key advisers, later known as [EXCOMM](/wiki/EXCOMM "EXCOMM"), that met secretly between October 16 and 28.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006207–208-226)

More than a third of [U.S. National Security Council](/wiki/U.S._National_Security_Council "U.S. National Security Council") (NSC) members favored an unannounced air assault on the missile sites, but some saw this as "[Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") in reverse."[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993387-227) There was some concern from the international community (asked in confidence) that the assault plan was an overreaction, given that Eisenhower had placed [PGM-19 Jupiter](/wiki/PGM-19_Jupiter "PGM-19 Jupiter") missiles in Italy and Turkey in 1958. It also could not be assured that the assault would be 100% effective.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993388-228) In concurrence with a majority vote of the NSC, Kennedy decided on a [naval blockade](/wiki/Naval_blockade "Naval blockade") (or "quarantine"). On October 22, after privately informing the cabinet and leading members of Congress about the situation, Kennedy announced the naval blockade on national television and warned that U.S. forces would seize "offensive weapons and associated materiel" that Soviet vessels might attempt to deliver to Cuba.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993389-229)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/President_confers_with_Attorney_General._Attorney_General_Kennedy%2C_President_Kennedy._White_House%2C_West_Wing_Collonade._-_NARA_-_194239.jpg/250px-President_confers_with_Attorney_General._Attorney_General_Kennedy%2C_President_Kennedy._White_House%2C_West_Wing_Collonade._-_NARA_-_194239.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_confers_with_Attorney_General._Attorney_General_Kennedy,_President_Kennedy._White_House,_West_Wing_Collonade._-_NARA_-_194239.jpg)

Kennedy confers with Attorney General [Robert Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_Kennedy "Robert Kennedy"); c. October 1962.

The U.S. Navy would stop and inspect all Soviet ships arriving off Cuba, beginning October 24. Several Soviet ships approached the blockade line, but they stopped or reversed course.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006220-230) The OAS gave unanimous support to the removal of the missiles. Kennedy exchanged two sets of letters with Khrushchev, to no avail.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993390-231) UN Secretary General [U Thant](/wiki/U_Thant "U Thant") requested both parties to reverse their decisions and enter a cooling-off period. Khrushchev agreed, but Kennedy did not.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993403-232) Kennedy managed to preserve restraint when a Soviet missile unauthorizedly downed a U.S. Lockheed U-2 reconnaissance aircraft over Cuba, killing pilot [Rudolf Anderson](/wiki/Rudolf_Anderson "Rudolf Anderson").[[231]](#cite_note-233)

At the president's direction, Robert Kennedy privately informed Soviet Ambassador [Anatoly Dobrynin](/wiki/Anatoly_Dobrynin "Anatoly Dobrynin") that the U.S. would remove the Jupiter missiles from Turkey "within a short time after this crisis was over."[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006225–226-234) On October 28, Khrushchev agreed to dismantle the missile sites, subject to UN inspections.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993426-235) The U.S. publicly promised never to invade Cuba and privately agreed to remove its Jupiter missiles from Italy and Turkey, which were by then obsolete and had been supplanted by submarines equipped with [UGM-27 Polaris](/wiki/UGM-27_Polaris "UGM-27 Polaris") missiles.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenney2000184–186-236)

In the aftermath, a [Moscow–Washington hotline](/wiki/Moscow%E2%80%93Washington_hotline "Moscow–Washington hotline") was established to ensure clear communications between the leaders of the two countries.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008723-237) This crisis brought the world closer to nuclear war than at any point before or after, but "the humanity" of Khrushchev and Kennedy prevailed.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenney2000189-238) The crisis improved the image of American willpower and the president's credibility. Kennedy's approval rating increased from 66% to 77% immediately thereafter.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993425-239)

#### Latin America and communism

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Latin America](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Latin_America "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

See also: [Alliance for Progress](/wiki/Alliance_for_Progress "Alliance for Progress")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Alliance_for_Progress_in_Venezuela_1961.jpg/250px-Alliance_for_Progress_in_Venezuela_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alliance_for_Progress_in_Venezuela_1961.jpg)

Kennedy in December 1961 promoting the [Alliance for Progress](/wiki/Alliance_for_Progress "Alliance for Progress") with Venezuelan President [Rómulo Betancourt](/wiki/R%C3%B3mulo_Betancourt "Rómulo Betancourt")

Believing that "those who make peaceful revolution impossible, will make violent revolution inevitable,"[[238]](#cite_note-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241) Kennedy sought to contain the perceived threat of communism in Latin America by establishing the [Alliance for Progress](/wiki/Alliance_for_Progress "Alliance for Progress"), which sent aid to some countries and sought greater [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") standards in the region.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2002788–789-242) In response to Kennedy's plea, Congress voted for an initial grant of $500 million in May 1961.[[241]](#cite_note-243) The Alliance for Progress supported the construction of housing, schools, airports, hospitals, clinics and water-purification projects as well as the distribution of free textbooks to students.[[242]](#cite_note-auto-244) However, the program did not meet many of its goals. Massive land reform was not achieved; populations more than kept pace with gains in health and welfare; and according to one study, only 2 percent of economic growth in 1960s Latin America directly benefited the poor.[[243]](#cite_note-245)[[244]](#cite_note-246) U.S. presidents after Kennedy were less supportive of the program and by 1973, the permanent committee established to implement the Alliance was disbanded by the OAS.[[242]](#cite_note-auto-244)

The Eisenhower administration, through the CIA, had begun formulating plans to assassinate Castro in Cuba and [Rafael Trujillo](/wiki/Rafael_Trujillo "Rafael Trujillo") in the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"). When Kennedy took office, he privately instructed the CIA that any plan must include [plausible deniability](/wiki/Plausible_deniability "Plausible deniability") by the U.S. His public position was in opposition.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993140–142-247) In June 1961, the Dominican Republic's leader was assassinated; in the days following, Undersecretary of State [Chester Bowles](/wiki/Chester_Bowles "Chester Bowles") led a cautious reaction by the nation. Robert Kennedy, who saw an opportunity for the U.S., called Bowles "a gutless bastard" to his face.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993152-248)

#### Laos

See also: [Laotian Civil War](/wiki/Laotian_Civil_War "Laotian Civil War")

After the election, Eisenhower emphasized to Kennedy that the communist threat in Southeast Asia required priority; Eisenhower considered [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") to be "the cork in the bottle" in regards to the regional threat.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199375-249) In March 1961, Kennedy voiced a change in policy from supporting a "free" Laos to a "neutral" Laos, indicating privately that [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") should be deemed America's tripwire for communism's spread in the area.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199375-249) Though he was unwilling to commit U.S. forces to a major military intervention in Laos, Kennedy did approve [CIA activities](/wiki/CIA_activities_in_Laos "CIA activities in Laos") designed to defeat Communist insurgents through bombing raids and the recruitment of the [Hmong people](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people").[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996498-250)

#### Vietnam

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Vietnam](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Vietnam "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

See also: [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Press_Conference%2C_State_Department_Auditorium%2C_JFKWHP-ST-C100-1-61.jpg/250px-Press_Conference%2C_State_Department_Auditorium%2C_JFKWHP-ST-C100-1-61.jpg)](/wiki/File:Press_Conference,_State_Department_Auditorium,_JFKWHP-ST-C100-1-61.jpg)

Kennedy speaking in a televised press conference on the situation in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), c. March 23, 1961

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/CBS_News_Anchor%2C_Walter_Cronkite%2C_Interviews_President_John_F._Kennedy.jpg/250px-CBS_News_Anchor%2C_Walter_Cronkite%2C_Interviews_President_John_F._Kennedy.jpg)](/wiki/File:CBS_News_Anchor,_Walter_Cronkite,_Interviews_President_John_F._Kennedy.jpg)

[Walter Cronkite](/wiki/Walter_Cronkite "Walter Cronkite") of [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News") interviewing Kennedy in [Cape Cod, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cape_Cod,_Massachusetts "Cape Cod, Massachusetts") on September 2, 1963, about [U.S. involvement in Vietnam](/wiki/United_States_in_the_Vietnam_War "United States in the Vietnam War")

During his presidency, Kennedy continued policies that provided political, economic, and military support to the [South Vietnamese](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam") government.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunniganNofi1999257-251) Vietnam had been divided into a communist North Vietnam and a non-communist South Vietnam after the [1954 Geneva Conference](/wiki/1954_Geneva_Conference "1954 Geneva Conference"), but Kennedy escalated American involvement in Vietnam in 1961 by financing the [South Vietnam army](/wiki/Army_of_the_Republic_of_Vietnam "Army of the Republic of Vietnam"), increasing the number of U.S. [military advisors](/wiki/Military_advisors "Military advisors") above the levels of the Eisenhower administration, and authorizing U.S. helicopter units to provide support to South Vietnamese forces.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006256–261-252) On January 18, 1962, Kennedy formally authorized escalated involvement when he signed the National Security Action Memorandum (NSAM) – "Subversive Insurgency (War of Liberation)."[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993281-253) [Operation Ranch Hand](/wiki/Operation_Ranch_Hand "Operation Ranch Hand"), a large-scale aerial defoliation effort using the herbicide [Agent Orange](/wiki/Agent_Orange "Agent Orange"), began on the roadsides of South Vietnam to combat [guerrilla defendants](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare").[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993259-254)[[253]](#cite_note-255)

Though Kennedy provided support for South Vietnam throughout his tenure, Vietnam remained a secondary issue for the Kennedy administration until 1963.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006256–257-256) On September 2, Kennedy declared in an interview with [Walter Cronkite](/wiki/Walter_Cronkite "Walter Cronkite") of [CBS](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News"):

> In the final analysis, it is their war. They are the ones who have to win it or lose it. We can help them, we can give them equipment, we can send our men out there as advisers, but they have to win it, the people of Vietnam, against the Communists... But I don't agree with those who say we should withdraw. That would be a great mistake... [The United States] made this effort to defend Europe. Now Europe is quite secure. We also have to participate—we may not like it—in the defense of Asia.[[255]](#cite_note-257)[[256]](#cite_note-258)

Kennedy increasingly soured on the president of South Vietnam, [Ngo Dinh Diem](/wiki/Ngo_Dinh_Diem "Ngo Dinh Diem"), whose violent [crackdown on Buddhist practices](/wiki/Buddhist_crisis "Buddhist crisis") galvanized opposition to his leadership. In August 1963, [Henry Cabot Lodge Jr.](/wiki/Henry_Cabot_Lodge_Jr. "Henry Cabot Lodge Jr.") replaced [Frederick Nolting](/wiki/Frederick_Nolting "Frederick Nolting") as the U.S. ambassador to South Vietnam. Days after his arrival in South Vietnam, Lodge reported that several South Vietnamese generals sought the assent of the U.S. government to their plan of removing Diem from power. The Kennedy administration was split regarding not just the removal of Diem, but also their assessment of the military situation and the proper U.S. role in the country. After the State Department sent a [diplomatic cable](/wiki/Cable_243 "Cable 243") to Lodge that ordered him to pressure Diem to remove military authority from his brother, [Ngô Đình Nhu](/wiki/Ng%C3%B4_%C4%90%C3%ACnh_Nhu "Ngô Đình Nhu"), or face potential withdrawal of U.S. support and removal from power,[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993562–563-259) Kennedy instructed Lodge to offer covert assistance to a coup d'état, excluding assassination.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993617-260) On November 1, 1963, a junta of senior military officers executed the [coup](/wiki/1963_South_Vietnamese_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1963 South Vietnamese coup d'état") which led to the [arrest and assassinations of Diem and Nhu](/wiki/Arrest_and_assassination_of_Ng%C3%B4_%C4%90%C3%ACnh_Di%E1%BB%87m "Arrest and assassination of Ngô Đình Diệm") on November 2.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006262–268-261)

By November 1963, there were 16,000 American military personnel in South Vietnam, up from Eisenhower's 900 advisors;[[260]](#cite_note-262) more than one hundred Americans had been killed in action and no final policy decision was made.[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2011393–394-265) In the aftermath of the aborted coup in September 1963, the Kennedy administration reevaluated its policies in South Vietnam. Kennedy rejected the full-scale deployment of ground soldiers but also the total withdrawal of U.S. forces.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006265–266-266) Historians disagree on whether the U.S. military presence in Vietnam would have escalated had Kennedy survived and been re-elected in 1964.[[265]](#cite_note-267) Fueling the debate are statements made by Secretary of Defense McNamara in the 2003 documentary film *[The Fog of War](/wiki/The_Fog_of_War "The Fog of War")* that Kennedy was strongly considering pulling out of Vietnam after the 1964 election,[[266]](#cite_note-268) and comments made by Kennedy administration [White House Counsel](/wiki/White_House_Counsel "White House Counsel") and speechwriter [Ted Sorensen](/wiki/Ted_Sorensen "Ted Sorensen") in a 2008 memoir suggesting that Kennedy was undecided about what policy direction to take.[[267]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2011393–394-265)

On October 11, 1963, Kennedy signed [NSAM 263](/wiki/National_Security_Action_Memorandum_263 "National Security Action Memorandum 263") ordering the withdrawal of 1,000 military personnel by the end of the year following the third recommendation of the [McNamara–Taylor mission](/wiki/McNamara%E2%80%93Taylor_mission "McNamara–Taylor mission") report, which concluded that the training program for the South Vietnamese military had sufficiently progressed to justify the withdrawal.[[268]](#cite_note-NSAM_263-270)[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003680-271)[[270]](#cite_note-McNamara-Taylor-272) However, NSAM 263 also approved the first recommendation of the report to continue providing support to South Vietnam to prevent the spread of communism and until the [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") was suppressed, while the third recommendation suggested that even if the majority of the U.S. military objective was completed by the end of 1965 that continued presence of U.S. training personnel in more limited numbers could be necessary if the insurgency was not suppressed.[[271]](#cite_note-273)[[272]](#cite_note-274)[[270]](#cite_note-McNamara-Taylor-272)

#### West Berlin speech

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/JFKBerlinSpeech.jpg/250px-JFKBerlinSpeech.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFKBerlinSpeech.jpg)

Kennedy delivering his speech in [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin")

In 1963, Germany was enduring a time of particular vulnerability due to Soviet aggression to the east as well as the impending retirement of West German Chancellor [Adenauer](/wiki/Adenauer "Adenauer").[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993534-275) At the same time, French President Charles de Gaulle was trying to build a Franco-West German counterweight to the American and Soviet spheres of influence.[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278) To Kennedy's eyes, this Franco-German cooperation seemed directed against [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")'s influence in Europe.[[277]](#cite_note-279)

To reinforce the U.S. alliance with West Germany, Kennedy travelled to West Germany and West Berlin in June 1963. On June 26, Kennedy toured West Berlin, culminating in a public speech at the city hall in front of hundreds of thousands of enthusiastic Berliners.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaum2008125–163-280) He reiterated the American commitment to Germany and criticized communism, and was met with an ecstatic response from the massive audience.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003624-281) Kennedy used the construction of the Berlin Wall as an example of the failures of communism: "Freedom has many difficulties, and democracy is not perfect. But we have never had to put a wall up to keep our people in, to prevent them from leaving us." The speech is known for its famous phrase *"[Ich bin ein Berliner](/wiki/Ich_bin_ein_Berliner "Ich bin ein Berliner")"* ("I am a Berliner").[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaum2008147–156-282)

#### Middle East

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Middle East](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Middle_East "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Kennedy_with_Shah_of_Iran%2C_1961.jpg/250px-Kennedy_with_Shah_of_Iran%2C_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kennedy_with_Shah_of_Iran,_1961.jpg)

Shah [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi") of Iran, Kennedy, and U.S. Defense Secretary [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara") in the [White House Cabinet Room](/wiki/Cabinet_Room_(White_House) "Cabinet Room (White House)") on April 13, 1962

Kennedy ended the arms embargo that the Truman and Eisenhower administrations had enforced on [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") in favor of increased security ties, becoming the founder of the [U.S.-Israeli military alliance](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_military_relations "Israel–United States military relations"). Describing the protection of Israel as a moral and national commitment, he was the first to introduce the concept of a 'special relationship' between the U.S. and Israel.[[281]](#cite_note-283) In 1962, the Kennedy administration sold Israel a major weapon system, the [Hawk antiaircraft missile](/wiki/MIM-23_Hawk "MIM-23 Hawk"). Historians differ as to whether Kennedy pursued security ties with Israel primarily to shore up support with Jewish-American voters or because he admired the Jewish state.[[282]](#cite_note-284)

In December 1961, [Abd al-Karim Qasim](/wiki/Abd_al-Karim_Qasim "Abd al-Karim Qasim")'s Iraqi government passed Public Law 80, which restricted the partially American-controlled [Iraq Petroleum Company](/wiki/Iraq_Petroleum_Company "Iraq Petroleum Company") (IPC)'s [concessionary holding](/wiki/Concessionary_holding "Concessionary holding") to those areas in which oil was actually being produced (namely, the fields at [Az Zubair](/wiki/Az_Zubair "Az Zubair") and [Kirkuk](/wiki/Kirkuk "Kirkuk")), effectively expropriating 99.5% of the IPC concession. British and U.S. officials demanded that the Kennedy administration place pressure on the Qasim regime.[[283]](#cite_note-285) In April 1962, the State Department issued new guidelines on Iraq that were intended to increase American influence. Meanwhile, Kennedy instructed the CIA—under the direction of [Archibald Bulloch Roosevelt Jr.](/wiki/Archibald_Bulloch_Roosevelt_Jr. "Archibald Bulloch Roosevelt Jr.")—to begin making preparations for a military coup against Qasim.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson201543–45-286)

The anti-imperialist and anti-communist [Iraqi Ba'ath Party](/wiki/Iraqi_Ba%27ath_Party "Iraqi Ba'ath Party") overthrew and executed Qasim in [a violent coup](/wiki/Ramadan_Revolution "Ramadan Revolution") on February 8, 1963. Despite persistent rumors that the CIA orchestrated the coup, declassified documents and the testimony of former CIA officers indicate that there was no direct American involvement.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson201545,_57–58-287) The Kennedy administration was pleased with the outcome and ultimately approved a $55-million arms deal for Iraq.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson201560–61,_80-288)

#### Ireland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/President%27s_Trip_to_Europe-_Motorcade_in_Cork._President_Kennedy%2C_motorcade%2C_spectators._Cork%2C_Ireland_-_NARA_-_194227.jpg/250px-President%27s_Trip_to_Europe-_Motorcade_in_Cork._President_Kennedy%2C_motorcade%2C_spectators._Cork%2C_Ireland_-_NARA_-_194227.jpg)](/wiki/File:President%27s_Trip_to_Europe-_Motorcade_in_Cork._President_Kennedy,_motorcade,_spectators._Cork,_Ireland_-_NARA_-_194227.jpg)

Kennedy's motorcade through [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"), Ireland on June 28, 1963

During his four-day visit to his ancestral home of Ireland beginning on June 26, 1963,[[287]](#cite_note-289) Kennedy accepted a grant of [armorial bearings](/wiki/Achievement_(heraldry) "Achievement (heraldry)") from the [Chief Herald of Ireland](/wiki/Chief_Herald_of_Ireland "Chief Herald of Ireland"), received honorary degrees from the [National University of Ireland](/wiki/National_University_of_Ireland "National University of Ireland") and [Trinity College Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin"), attended a State Dinner in Dublin, and was conferred with the freedom of the towns and cities of Wexford, Cork, Dublin, Galway, and Limerick.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESorensen1966656-290)[[289]](#cite_note-homecoming-291) He visited the cottage at Dunganstown, near [New Ross](/wiki/New_Ross "New Ross"), County Wexford, where his ancestors had lived before emigrating to America.[[290]](#cite_note-BBC_1963-292)

Kennedy was the first foreign leader to address the [Houses of the Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas "Oireachtas"), the Irish parliament.[[289]](#cite_note-homecoming-291)[[291]](#cite_note-293)[[292]](#cite_note-294) Kennedy later told aides that the trip was the best four days of his life.[[293]](#cite_note-295)

#### American University speech

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[*World Peace* Speech](/wiki/File:Jfk_American_University_4654_06-10-63.ogg "File:Jfk American University 4654 06-10-63.ogg")

Speech from [American University](/wiki/American_University "American University") by John F. Kennedy, June 10, 1963 (duration 26:47)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

On June 10, 1963, Kennedy, at the high point of his rhetorical powers,[[294]](#cite_note-296) delivered the [commencement address at American University](/wiki/American_University_speech "American University speech"). Also known as "A Strategy of Peace", not only did Kennedy outline a plan to curb nuclear arms, but he also "laid out a hopeful, yet realistic route for world peace at a time when the U.S. and Soviet Union faced the potential for an escalating [nuclear arms race](/wiki/Nuclear_arms_race "Nuclear arms race")."[[295]](#cite_note-297) Kennedy also announced that the Soviets had expressed a desire to negotiate a nuclear test ban treaty, and that the U.S. had postponed planned atmospheric tests.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993514-298)

#### Nuclear Test Ban Treaty

Main article: [Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty](/wiki/Partial_Nuclear_Test_Ban_Treaty "Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/President_Kennedy_signs_Nuclear_Test_Ban_Treaty%2C_07_October_1963.jpg/250px-President_Kennedy_signs_Nuclear_Test_Ban_Treaty%2C_07_October_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Kennedy_signs_Nuclear_Test_Ban_Treaty,_07_October_1963.jpg)

Kennedy signs the [Partial Test Ban Treaty](/wiki/Partial_Test_Ban_Treaty "Partial Test Ban Treaty"), a major milestone in early [nuclear disarmament](/wiki/Nuclear_disarmament "Nuclear disarmament"), on October 7, 1963.

Troubled by the long-term dangers of [radioactive contamination](/wiki/Radioactive_contamination "Radioactive contamination") and [nuclear proliferation](/wiki/Nuclear_proliferation "Nuclear proliferation"), Kennedy and Khrushchev agreed to negotiate a nuclear test ban treaty, originally conceived in Adlai Stevenson's 1956 presidential campaign.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993552-299) In their Vienna summit meeting in June 1961, Khrushchev and Kennedy reached an informal understanding against nuclear testing, but the Soviet Union began testing nuclear weapons that September. In response, the United States conducted tests five days later.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993227-300) Shortly afterwards, new U.S. satellites began delivering images that made it clear that the Soviets were substantially behind the U.S. in the arms race.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993229-301) Nevertheless, the greater nuclear strength of the U.S. was of little value as long as the Soviet Union perceived itself to be at parity.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993243-302)

In July 1963, Kennedy sent [W. Averell Harriman](/wiki/W._Averell_Harriman "W. Averell Harriman") to Moscow to negotiate a treaty with the Soviets.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993542-303) The introductory sessions included Khrushchev, who later delegated Soviet representation to [Andrei Gromyko](/wiki/Andrei_Gromyko "Andrei Gromyko"). It quickly became clear that a comprehensive test ban would not be implemented, due largely to the reluctance of the Soviets to allow inspections to verify compliance.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993548-304)

Ultimately, the United States, the United Kingdom, and the Soviet Union were the initial signatories to a limited treaty, which prohibited atomic testing on the ground, in the atmosphere, or underwater, but not underground. The U.S. Senate approved the treaty on September 23, 1963, and Kennedy signed it on October 7, 1963.[[303]](#cite_note-305) France was quick to declare that it was free to continue developing and testing its nuclear defenses.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993550-306)

### Domestic policy

#### New Frontier

Main article: [New Frontier](/wiki/New_Frontier "New Frontier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Bill_Signing-_Manpower_Development_and_Training_Act_of_1962._President_Kennedy%2C_Secretary_of_Labor_Arthur_Goldberg..._-_NARA_-_194205.jpg/250px-Bill_Signing-_Manpower_Development_and_Training_Act_of_1962._President_Kennedy%2C_Secretary_of_Labor_Arthur_Goldberg..._-_NARA_-_194205.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Signing-_Manpower_Development_and_Training_Act_of_1962._President_Kennedy,_Secretary_of_Labor_Arthur_Goldberg..._-_NARA_-_194205.jpg)

Kennedy signing the [Manpower Development and Training Act](/wiki/New_Frontier#Unemployment "New Frontier"), March 15, 1962

Kennedy called his domestic proposals the "[New Frontier](/wiki/New_Frontier "New Frontier")".[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrinkley201263–65-307) However, Kennedy's small margin of victory in the 1960 election, his lack of deep connections to influential members of Congress, and his administration's focus on foreign policy hindered the passage of New Frontier policies.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio200640–41,_100-308)

In 1961, Kennedy prioritized passing five bills: federal assistance for education, medical insurance for the elderly, housing legislation, federal aid to struggling areas, and an increase in the federal minimum wage.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio200699-309) Kennedy's bill to increase the [federal minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States") to $1.25 an hour passed in early 1961, but an amendment inserted by conservative leader from Georgia, [Carl Vinson](/wiki/Carl_Vinson "Carl Vinson"), exempted laundry workers from the law.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006102–103-310) Kennedy also won passage of the [Area Redevelopment Act](/wiki/Area_Redevelopment_Administration "Area Redevelopment Administration") and the Housing Act of 1961. The Area Redevelopment Act, a $394 million program, provided federal funding to economically struggling regions (primarily in [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia")), while the Housing Act of 1961 provided funds for [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") and [public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing") and authorized federal [mortgage loans](/wiki/Mortgage_loans "Mortgage loans") to those who did not qualify for public housing.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006105–106-311) Kennedy proposed a bill providing for $2.3 billion in federal educational aid to the states, with more money going to states with lower [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income"). Though the Senate passed the education bill, it was defeated in the House by a coalition of Republicans, Southern Democrats, and Catholics.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006103–104-312) Kennedy's health insurance bill, which would have paid for hospitalization and nursing costs for the elderly, failed to pass either house of Congress.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006104–105-313) A bill that would have established the [Department of Urban Affairs and Housing](/wiki/Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "Department of Housing and Urban Development") was also defeated.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006106–107-314)

In 1962, Kennedy won approval of the [Manpower Development and Training Act](/wiki/Manpower_Development_and_Training_Act "Manpower Development and Training Act"), a three-year program aimed at retraining workers displaced by new technology. Its impact on [structural unemployment](/wiki/Structural_unemployment "Structural unemployment"), however, was minimal.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006105–106-311) At the urging of his sister [Eunice](/wiki/Eunice_Kennedy_Shriver "Eunice Kennedy Shriver"), Kennedy made [intellectual disabilities](/wiki/Intellectual_disabilities "Intellectual disabilities") a priority for his administration. In 1963, Congress passed the [Community Mental Health Act](/wiki/Community_Mental_Health_Act "Community Mental Health Act"), which provided funding to local mental health community centers and research facilities.[[313]](#cite_note-315)

Trade policy included both domestic and foreign policy. The 1962 [Trade Expansion Act](/wiki/Trade_Expansion_Act "Trade Expansion Act") was passed by Congress with wide majorities. It authorized the president to negotiate tariff reductions on a reciprocal basis of up to 50 percent with the [European Common Market](/wiki/European_Common_Market "European Common Market").[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006107–109-316) The legislation paved the way for the [Kennedy Round](/wiki/Kennedy_Round "Kennedy Round") of [General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade](/wiki/General_Agreement_on_Tariffs_and_Trade "General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade") negotiations, concluding on June 30, 1967, the last day before expiration of the Act.[[315]](#cite_note-317)

#### Taxes

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Taxes and the Treasury](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Taxes_and_the_Treasury "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

[Walter Heller](/wiki/Walter_Heller "Walter Heller"), who served as the chairman of the CEA, advocated for a [Keynesian](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics")-style tax cut designed to help spur economic growth, and Kennedy adopted this policy.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996464–465-318) The idea was that a tax cut would stimulate consumer demand, which in turn would lead to higher economic growth, lower unemployment, and increased federal revenues.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006125-319) To the disappointment of liberals like [John Kenneth Galbraith](/wiki/John_Kenneth_Galbraith "John Kenneth Galbraith"), Kennedy's embrace of the tax cut shifted his administration's focus away from the proposed old-age health insurance program and other domestic expenditures.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006136–137-320) In January 1963, Kennedy proposed a tax cut that would reduce the top marginal tax rate from 91 to 65 percent, and lower the corporate tax rate from 52 to 47 percent. The predictions according to the Keynesian model indicated the cuts would decrease income taxes by about $10 billion and corporate taxes by about $3.5 billion. The plan included reforms designed to reduce the impact of [itemized deductions](/wiki/Itemized_deduction "Itemized deduction"), as well as provisions to help the elderly and handicapped. Republicans and many Southern Democrats opposed the bill, calling for simultaneous reductions in expenditures, but debate continued throughout 1963.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006139–141-321) Three months after Kennedy died, Johnson pushed the plan through Congress. The [Revenue Act of 1964](/wiki/Revenue_Act_of_1964 "Revenue Act of 1964") lowered the top individual rate to 70 percent, and the top corporate rate to 48 percent.[[320]](#cite_note-322)

#### Economy

See also: [Recession of 1960–1961](/wiki/Recession_of_1960%E2%80%931961 "Recession of 1960–1961") and [Kennedy Slide of 1962](/wiki/Kennedy_Slide_of_1962 "Kennedy Slide of 1962")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/JFK_delivers_State_of_the_Union_Address%2C_14_January_1963.jpg/250px-JFK_delivers_State_of_the_Union_Address%2C_14_January_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_delivers_State_of_the_Union_Address,_14_January_1963.jpg)

President Kennedy delivers his State of the Union Address; c. January 14, 1963.

Kennedy ended a period of tight fiscal policies, loosening monetary policy to keep [interest rates](/wiki/Interest_rate "Interest rate") down and to encourage growth of the economy.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000293-323) He presided over the first government budget to top the $100 billion mark, in 1962, and his first budget in 1961 resulted in the nation's first non-war, non-recession [deficit](/wiki/Government_budget_deficit "Government budget deficit").[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000324-324) The economy, which had been through two recessions in three years and was in one when Kennedy took office, accelerated notably throughout his administration. Despite low [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") and interest rates, the [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") had grown by an average of only 2.2% per annum during the Eisenhower administration (scarcely more than population growth at the time), and it had declined by 1% during Eisenhower's last twelve months in office.[[323]](#cite_note-Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis-325)

The economy turned around and prospered during Kennedy's presidency. The GDP expanded by an average of 5.5% from early 1961 to late 1963,[[323]](#cite_note-Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis-325) while inflation remained steady at around 1% and unemployment eased.[[324]](#cite_note-GDP_1913_to_2002-326) Industrial production rose by 15% and motor vehicle sales increased by 40%.[[325]](#cite_note-Statistical_Abstract_1964-327) This sustained rate of growth in GDP and industry continued until around 1969.[[323]](#cite_note-Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis-325)

Kennedy was proud that his Labor Department helped keep wages steady in the steel industry, but was outraged in April 1962 when [Roger Blough](/wiki/Roger_Blough "Roger Blough"), the president of [U.S. Steel](/wiki/U.S._Steel "U.S. Steel"), quietly informed Kennedy that his company would raise prices.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParmet1983238-328) In response, Attorney General Robert Kennedy began a [price-fixing](/wiki/Price-fixing "Price-fixing") investigation against U.S. Steel, and President Kennedy convinced other steel companies to rescind their price increases until finally even U.S. Steel, isolated and in danger of being undersold, agreed to rescind its own price increase.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006130–134-329) An editorial in *The New York Times* praised Kennedy's actions and stated that the steel industry's price increase "imperil[ed] the economic welfare of the country by inviting a tidal wave of inflation."[[328]](#cite_note-NY_Times_1962-330) Nevertheless, the administration's Bureau of Budget reported the price increase would have caused a net gain for the GDP as well as a net budget surplus.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993300-331) The stock market, which had steadily declined since Kennedy's election in 1960, dropped 10% shortly after the administration's action on the steel industry took place.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993318–320-332)

#### Civil rights movement

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Civil rights](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Civil_rights "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

See also: [Civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Thurgood_Marshall_1957-09-17.jpg/250px-Thurgood_Marshall_1957-09-17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thurgood_Marshall_1957-09-17.jpg)

In May 1961, Kennedy appointed [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall") to the [U.S. Court of Appeals](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit").

Kennedy verbally supported [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") during his 1960 presidential campaign; he telephoned [Coretta Scott King](/wiki/Coretta_Scott_King "Coretta Scott King"), wife of [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr."), who had been jailed while trying to integrate a department store lunch counter. Robert Kennedy called Georgia Governor [Ernest Vandiver](/wiki/Ernest_Vandiver "Ernest Vandiver") and obtained King's release from prison, which drew additional Black support to his brother's candidacy.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003292–293-333) Recognizing that conservative Southern Democrats could block legislation, Kennedy did not introduce civil rights legislation on taking office.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002487-334) He needed their support to pass his economic and foreign policy agendas, and to support his reelection in 1964.[[333]](#cite_note-335) Kennedy did appoint many Blacks to office, including civil rights attorney [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall") to the [U.S. Court of Appeals](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit").[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) [Abraham Bolden](/wiki/Abraham_Bolden "Abraham Bolden"), the first black [Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service") agent, was appointed by Kennedy.[[335]](#cite_note-337)

Kennedy believed the grassroots movement for civil rights would anger many Southern Whites and make it more difficult to pass civil rights laws in Congress, and he distanced himself from it.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant2006a60,_66-338) As articulated by Robert Kennedy, the administration's early priority was to "keep the president out of this civil rights mess."[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993126-339) Civil rights movement participants, mainly those on the front line in the South, viewed Kennedy as lukewarm,[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) especially concerning the [Freedom Riders](/wiki/Freedom_Riders "Freedom Riders"). In May 1961, the [Congress of Racial Equality](/wiki/Congress_of_Racial_Equality "Congress of Racial Equality"), led by [James Farmer](/wiki/James_Farmer "James Farmer"), organized integrated Freedom Rides to test a Supreme Court case ruling that declared segregation on interstate transportation illegal.[[338]](#cite_note-340) The Riders were repeatedly met with mob violence, including by federal and state law enforcement officers.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) Kennedy assigned [federal marshals](/wiki/Federal_marshal "Federal marshal") to protect the Riders rather than using federal troops or uncooperative FBI agents.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) Kennedy feared sending federal troops would stir up "hated memories of [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_Era "Reconstruction Era")" among conservative Southern whites.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) The Justice Department then petitioned the [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission") (ICC) to adhere to federal law. By September 1961, the ICC ruled in favor of the petition.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

On March 6, 1961, Kennedy signed [Executive Order 10925](/wiki/Executive_Order_10925 "Executive Order 10925"), which required government contractors to "take affirmative action to ensure that applicants are employed and that employees are treated during employment without regard to their race, creed, color, or national origin."[[340]](#cite_note-342) It established the [President's Committee on Equal Employment Opportunity](/wiki/Equal_Employment_Opportunity_Commission#Background "Equal Employment Opportunity Commission").[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996473–475-343)

In September 1962, [James Meredith](/wiki/James_Meredith "James Meredith") enrolled at the all-White [University of Mississippi](/wiki/University_of_Mississippi "University of Mississippi") but was prevented from entering. In response, Attorney General Robert Kennedy sent 400 federal marshals.[[342]](#cite_note-344) The [Ole Miss riot of 1962](/wiki/Ole_Miss_riot_of_1962 "Ole Miss riot of 1962") left two dead and dozens injured, prompting Kennedy to send in 3,000 troops to quell the riot.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant2006a71-345) Meredith did finally enroll in class. Kennedy regretted not sending in troops earlier and he began to doubt whether the "evils of Reconstruction" he had been taught or believed were true.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002490-336) On November 20, 1962, Kennedy signed [Executive Order 11063](/wiki/Executive_Order_11063 "Executive Order 11063"), which prohibited racial discrimination in federally supported housing.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003580-346)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/President_Kennedy_addresses_nation_on_Civil_Rights%2C_11_June_1963.jpg/250px-President_Kennedy_addresses_nation_on_Civil_Rights%2C_11_June_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Kennedy_addresses_nation_on_Civil_Rights,_11_June_1963.jpg)

Kennedy's [Report to the American People on Civil Rights](/wiki/Report_to_the_American_People_on_Civil_Rights "Report to the American People on Civil Rights"), c. June 11, 1963

On June 11, 1963, Kennedy intervened when Alabama Governor [George Wallace](/wiki/George_Wallace "George Wallace") blocked the [doorway](/wiki/Stand_in_the_Schoolhouse_Door "Stand in the Schoolhouse Door") to the [University of Alabama](/wiki/University_of_Alabama "University of Alabama") to stop two Black students, [Vivian Malone](/wiki/Vivian_Malone "Vivian Malone") and [James Hood](/wiki/James_Hood "James Hood"), from attending. Wallace moved aside only after being confronted by Deputy Attorney General [Nicholas Katzenbach](/wiki/Nicholas_Katzenbach "Nicholas Katzenbach") and the [Alabama National Guard](/wiki/Alabama_National_Guard "Alabama National Guard"), which had just been federalized by order of the president. That evening Kennedy gave his famous [Report to the American People on Civil Rights](/wiki/Report_to_the_American_People_on_Civil_Rights "Report to the American People on Civil Rights") speech on national television and radio, launching his initiative for civil rights legislation—to provide equal access to public schools and other facilities, and greater protection of voting rights.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993521–523-347)

His proposals became part of the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"). The day ended with the murder of an NAACP leader, [Medgar Evers](/wiki/Medgar_Evers "Medgar Evers"), in Mississippi.[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchlesinger2002966-348) As Kennedy had predicted, the day after his TV speech, and in reaction to it, House Majority leader [Carl Albert](/wiki/Carl_Albert "Carl Albert") called to advise him that his two-year signature effort in Congress to combat poverty in Appalachia had been defeated, primarily by the votes of Southern Democrats and Republicans.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993524-349) When Arthur Schlesinger Jr. complimented Kennedy on his remarks, Kennedy bitterly replied, "Yes, and look at what happened to area development the very next day in the House." He then added, "But of course, I had to give that speech, and I'm glad that I did."[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen2016357-350) On June 16, *The New York Times* published an editorial which argued that while Kennedy had initially "moved too slowly and with little evidence of deep moral commitment" in regards to civil rights he "now demonstrate[d] a genuine sense of urgency about eradicating racial discrimination from our national life."[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoduti2012206-351)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/John_F._Kennedy_and_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Meet_with_Organizers_of_%22March_on_Washington%22.jpg/250px-John_F._Kennedy_and_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Meet_with_Organizers_of_%22March_on_Washington%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_F._Kennedy_and_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Meet_with_Organizers_of_%22March_on_Washington%22.jpg)

Kennedy meetings with leaders of the [March on Washington](/wiki/March_on_Washington "March on Washington") in the Oval Office, c. August 28, 1963

A crowd of over 250,000, predominantly African Americans, gathered in Washington for the civil rights [March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom](/wiki/March_on_Washington_for_Jobs_and_Freedom "March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom") on August 28, 1963. Kennedy initially opposed the march, fearing it would have a negative effect on the prospects for the civil rights bills pending in Congress. These fears were heightened just prior to the march when FBI Director [J. Edgar Hoover](/wiki/J._Edgar_Hoover "J. Edgar Hoover") presented Kennedy with reports that some of King's close advisers, specifically [Jack O'Dell](/wiki/Jack_O%27Dell "Jack O'Dell") and [Stanley Levison](/wiki/Stanley_Levison "Stanley Levison"), were communists.[[350]](#cite_note-352) When King ignored the administration's warning, Robert Kennedy authorized the FBI to [wiretap](/wiki/Wiretap "Wiretap") King and other leaders of the [Southern Christian Leadership Conference](/wiki/Southern_Christian_Leadership_Conference "Southern Christian Leadership Conference").[[351]](#cite_note-353) Although Kennedy only gave written approval for limited wiretapping of King's phones "on a trial basis, for a month or so,"[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerst2007372-354) Hoover extended the clearance so his men were "unshackled" to look for evidence in any areas of King's life they deemed worthy.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerst2007372–374-355)

The Department of Justice was assigned to coordinate the federal government's involvement in the March on Washington on August 28; several hundred thousand dollars were channeled to the six sponsors of the March.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993580–584-356) To ensure a peaceful demonstration, the organizers and the president personally edited speeches that were inflammatory and collaborated on all aspects related to times and venues. Thousands of troops were placed on standby. Kennedy watched King's speech on TV and was very impressed. The March was considered a "triumph of managed protest," and not one arrest relating to the demonstration occurred. Afterwards, the March leaders accepted an invitation to the White House to meet with Kennedy, and photos were taken. Kennedy felt that the March was a victory for him as well and bolstered the chances for his civil rights bill.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993580–584-356)

Three weeks later, on Sunday, September 15, [a bomb exploded](/wiki/16th_Street_Baptist_Church_bombing "16th Street Baptist Church bombing") at the 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham; by the end of the day, four Black children had died in the explosion, and two others were shot to death in the aftermath.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993599–600-357) Due to this resurgent violence, the civil rights legislation underwent some drastic amendments that critically endangered any prospects for passage of the bill, to the outrage of Kennedy. He called the congressional leaders to the White House, and by the following day, the original bill, without the additions, had enough votes to get it out of the House committee.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993628–631-358) Gaining Republican support, Senator [Everett Dirksen](/wiki/Everett_Dirksen "Everett Dirksen") promised the legislation would be brought to a vote preventing a [Senate filibuster](/wiki/Filibuster_in_the_United_States_Senate "Filibuster in the United States Senate").[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002492-359) On July 2, 1964, the guarantees Kennedy proposed in his June 1963 speech became federal law, when President Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002492-359)

#### Status of women

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/American_Association_of_University_Women_members_with_President_John_F._Kennedy_as_he_signs_the_Equal_Pay_Act_into_law.jpg/250px-American_Association_of_University_Women_members_with_President_John_F._Kennedy_as_he_signs_the_Equal_Pay_Act_into_law.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Association_of_University_Women_members_with_President_John_F._Kennedy_as_he_signs_the_Equal_Pay_Act_into_law.jpg)

Kennedy signing the [Equal Pay Act of 1963](/wiki/Equal_Pay_Act_of_1963 "Equal Pay Act of 1963") into law

During the 1960 presidential campaign, Kennedy endorsed the concept of [equal pay for equal work](/wiki/Equal_pay_for_equal_work "Equal pay for equal work").[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006142-360) In December 1961, Kennedy signed an executive order creating the [Presidential Commission on the Status of Women](/wiki/Presidential_Commission_on_the_Status_of_Women "Presidential Commission on the Status of Women") to advise him on issues concerning the status of women.[[359]](#cite_note-361) Former First Lady Eleanor Roosevelt led the commission. The commission's final report was issued in October 1963; it documented the legal and cultural discrimination women in America faced and made several policy recommendations to bring about change.[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993433-362) On June 10, 1963, Kennedy signed the [Equal Pay Act of 1963](/wiki/Equal_Pay_Act_of_1963 "Equal Pay Act of 1963"), which amended the [Fair Labor Standards Act](/wiki/Fair_Labor_Standards_Act "Fair Labor Standards Act") and abolished wage disparity based on sex.[[361]](#cite_note-363)

#### Crime

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Crime](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Crime "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

Under the leadership of the attorney general, the Kennedy administration shifted the focus of the Justice Department, the FBI, and the IRS to [organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime"). Kennedy won congressional approval for five bills (i.e., [Federal Wire Act](/wiki/Federal_Wire_Act "Federal Wire Act") of 1961) designed to crack down on interstate [racketeering](/wiki/Racket_(crime) "Racket (crime)"), gambling, and the transportation of firearms.[[362]](#cite_note-364)[[363]](#cite_note-365)

On March 22, 1962, Kennedy signed into law a bill abolishing the mandatory death penalty for [first degree murder](/wiki/First_degree_murder_in_the_United_States "First degree murder in the United States") in the District of Columbia, the only remaining jurisdiction in the United States with such a penalty.[[364]](#cite_note-JFKlibrary.org_leg-366) The death penalty has not been applied in D.C. since 1957 and has now been abolished.[[365]](#cite_note-367)

#### Agriculture

Kennedy had relatively little interest in agricultural issues, but he sought to remedy the issue of overproduction, boost the income of farmers, and lower federal expenditures on agriculture. Under the direction of Secretary of Agriculture [Orville Freeman](/wiki/Orville_Freeman "Orville Freeman"), the administration sought to limit the production of farmers, but these proposals were generally defeated in Congress. To increase demand for domestic agricultural products and help the impoverished, Kennedy launched a pilot [Food Stamp program](/wiki/Supplemental_Nutrition_Assistance_Program "Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program") and expanded the [federal school lunch program](/wiki/National_School_Lunch_Act "National School Lunch Act").[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006109–118-368)

#### Native American relations

Further information: [Kinzua Dam § Native Americans](/wiki/Kinzua_Dam#Native_Americans "Kinzua Dam"), and [Seneca nation § Kinzua Dam](/wiki/Seneca_nation#Kinzua_Dam "Seneca nation")

Construction of the [Kinzua Dam](/wiki/Kinzua_Dam "Kinzua Dam") flooded 10,000 acres (4,000 hectares) of [Seneca nation](/wiki/Seneca_nation "Seneca nation") land that they had occupied under the [Treaty of 1794](/wiki/Treaty_of_Canandaigua "Treaty of Canandaigua"), and forced 600 Seneca to relocate to [Salamanca, New York](/wiki/Salamanca_(town),_New_York "Salamanca (town), New York"). Kennedy was asked by the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union") to halt the project, but he declined, citing a critical need for flood control. He expressed concern about the plight of the Seneca and directed government agencies to assist in obtaining more land, damages, and assistance to mitigate their displacement.[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBilharz200255-369)[[368]](#cite_note-Kennedy_letter_1961-370)

#### Space policy

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Space policy](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Space_policy "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

See also: [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") and [Space policy of the United States](/wiki/Space_policy_of_the_United_States "Space policy of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Werner_Von_Braun_and_President_John_F._Kennedy_at_Cape_Canaveral_Missile_Test_Annex_-_1963_-_63PC-0095.jpg/250px-Werner_Von_Braun_and_President_John_F._Kennedy_at_Cape_Canaveral_Missile_Test_Annex_-_1963_-_63PC-0095.jpg)](/wiki/File:Werner_Von_Braun_and_President_John_F._Kennedy_at_Cape_Canaveral_Missile_Test_Annex_-_1963_-_63PC-0095.jpg)

[Wernher von Braun](/wiki/Wernher_von_Braun "Wernher von Braun") explains the Saturn system to President Kennedy during his tour at the [Cape Canaveral Missile Test Annex](/wiki/Cape_Canaveral_Space_Force_Station "Cape Canaveral Space Force Station") on November 16, 1963

In the aftermath of the Soviet launch of *[Sputnik 1](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1")*, the first artificial Earth satellite, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") proposed a crewed [lunar landing](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing") by the early 1970s.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006150–151-371) Funding for the program, known as the [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program"), was far from certain as Eisenhower held an ambivalent attitude.[[370]](#cite_note-372) Early in his presidency, Kennedy was poised to dismantle the crewed space program, but he postponed any decision out of deference to Vice President Johnson, who had been a strong supporter of the program in the Senate.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993138-373) With [Jerome Wiesner](/wiki/Jerome_Wiesner "Jerome Wiesner"), Johnson was given a major role in overseeing the administration's space policy, and at Johnson's recommendation, Kennedy appointed [James E. Webb](/wiki/James_E._Webb "James E. Webb") to head NASA.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006151–152-374)

In Kennedy's [State of the Union](/wiki/State_of_the_Union "State of the Union") address in 1961, he suggested international cooperation in space. Khrushchev declined, as the Soviets did not wish to reveal the status of their rocketry and space capabilities.[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003502-375) In April 1961, Soviet cosmonaut [Yuri Gagarin](/wiki/Yuri_Gagarin "Yuri Gagarin") became the first person to fly in space, reinforcing American fears about being left behind by the Soviet Union.[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003393-376) Less than a month later, [Alan Shepard](/wiki/Alan_Shepard "Alan Shepard") became the first American to travel into space, strengthening Kennedy's confidence in NASA.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006152–153-377) The following year, [John Glenn](/wiki/John_Glenn "John Glenn"), aboard the [Mercury](/wiki/Project_Mercury "Project Mercury") craft *[Friendship 7](/wiki/Friendship_7 "Friendship 7")*, became the first American to orbit the Earth.[[376]](#cite_note-378)

In the aftermath of Gagarin's flight, as well as the failed Bay of Pigs invasion, Kennedy felt pressured to respond to the perceived erosion of American prestige. He asked Johnson to explore the feasibility of beating the Soviets to the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"). Though he was concerned about the program's costs, Kennedy agreed to Johnson's recommendation that the U.S. commit to a crewed lunar landing as the major objective of the space program. In a May 25 speech to Congress, Kennedy declared,[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006152–153-377)

> ... I believe that this nation should commit itself to achieving the goal, before this decade is out, of landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely to the Earth. No single space project in this period will be more impressive to mankind, or more important for the long-range exploration of space; and none will be so difficult or expensive to accomplish.[[377]](#cite_note-Kennedy_at_Congress_1961-379)[[378]](#cite_note-380)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/John_F._Kennedy_speaks_at_Rice_University.jpg/250px-John_F._Kennedy_speaks_at_Rice_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_F._Kennedy_speaks_at_Rice_University.jpg)

Kennedy speaks at [Rice University](/wiki/Rice_University "Rice University") on September 12, 1962

Though Gallup polling showed that many in the public were skeptical of the necessity of the Apollo program,[[379]](#cite_note-381) members of Congress were strongly supportive in 1961 and approved a major increase in NASA's funding. Webb began reorganizing NASA, increasing its staffing level, and building two new centers: a [Launch Operations Center](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center "Kennedy Space Center") for the [large Moon rocket](/wiki/Saturn_V "Saturn V") northwest of [Cape Canaveral Air Force Station](/wiki/Cape_Canaveral_Air_Force_Station "Cape Canaveral Air Force Station"), and a [Manned Spacecraft Center](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_Space_Center "Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center") in Houston. Kennedy took the latter occasion as an opportunity to deliver another [speech](/wiki/We_choose_to_go_to_the_Moon "We choose to go to the Moon") promoting the space effort on September 12, 1962, in which he said:

> No nation which expects to be the leader of other nations can expect to stay behind in this race for space. ... We choose to go to the Moon in this decade and do the other things, not because they are easy, but because they are hard.[[380]](#cite_note-Kennedy_at_Rice_1961-382)[[381]](#cite_note-383)

On November 21, 1962, in a cabinet meeting with Webb and other officials, Kennedy explained that the Moon shot was important for reasons of international prestige, and that the expense was justified.[[382]](#cite_note-384) On July 20, 1969, almost six years after Kennedy's death, [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") landed the first crewed spacecraft on the Moon.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006153–155-385)

### Judicial appointments

Main articles: [John F. Kennedy Supreme Court candidates](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Supreme_Court_candidates "John F. Kennedy Supreme Court candidates") and [List of federal judges appointed by John F. Kennedy](/wiki/List_of_federal_judges_appointed_by_John_F._Kennedy "List of federal judges appointed by John F. Kennedy")

In 1962, Kennedy appointed justices [Byron White](/wiki/Byron_White "Byron White") and [Arthur Goldberg](/wiki/Arthur_Goldberg "Arthur Goldberg") to the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States").[[384]](#cite_note-386) Additionally, Kennedy appointed 21 judges to the [United States Courts of Appeals](/wiki/United_States_Courts_of_Appeals "United States Courts of Appeals"), and 102 judges to the [United States district courts](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court").[[385]](#cite_note-387)

## Assassination

Main article: [Assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of the John F. Kennedy assassination](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_John_F._Kennedy_assassination "Timeline of the John F. Kennedy assassination").

Kennedy was assassinated in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") at 12:30 p.m. [Central Standard Time](/wiki/Central_Standard_Time "Central Standard Time") on November 22, 1963.[[386]](#cite_note-388) He was in Texas on a political trip to smooth over frictions in the Democratic Party between liberals [Ralph Yarborough](/wiki/Ralph_Yarborough "Ralph Yarborough") and [Don Yarborough](/wiki/Don_Yarborough "Don Yarborough") (no relation) and conservative [John Connally](/wiki/John_Connally "John Connally").[[387]](#cite_note-389) While traveling in a presidential [motorcade](/wiki/Motorcade "Motorcade") through [Dealey Plaza](/wiki/Dealey_Plaza "Dealey Plaza"), he was shot once in the back, the bullet exiting via his throat, and once in the head.[[388]](#cite_note-390)[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003694-391)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/JFK_Motorcade_GettyImages-517330536.jpg/250px-JFK_Motorcade_GettyImages-517330536.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_Motorcade_GettyImages-517330536.jpg)

The Kennedys and the Connallys in the presidential limousine moments before [the assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") in Dallas

Kennedy was taken to [Parkland Hospital](/wiki/Parkland_Hospital "Parkland Hospital"), where he was pronounced dead 30 minutes later, at 1:00 p.m.[[390]](#cite_note-auto3-392) He was 46 years old. [Lee Harvey Oswald](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald") was arrested for the murder of police officer [J. D. Tippit](/wiki/J._D._Tippit "J. D. Tippit") and was subsequently charged with Kennedy's assassination.[[391]](#cite_note-393) Oswald denied shooting anyone, claiming he was a [patsy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/patsy "wikt:patsy").[[392]](#cite_note-394)[[393]](#cite_note-395) On November 24, before he could be prosecuted, he was shot and killed by [Jack Ruby](/wiki/Jack_Ruby "Jack Ruby").[[390]](#cite_note-auto3-392) Ruby was arrested and convicted for the murder of Oswald. Ruby successfully appealed his conviction but died of cancer on January 3, 1967, while the date for his new trial was being set.[[394]](#cite_note-396)

President Johnson quickly issued an [executive order](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") to create the [Warren Commission](/wiki/Warren_Commission "Warren Commission")—chaired by Chief Justice [Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren")—to investigate the assassination. The commission concluded that Oswald acted alone in killing Kennedy and that Oswald was not part of any conspiracy.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrauer2002497-397)[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003698-398) These conclusions are disputed by many.[[397]](#cite_note-Russo-399) A [Gallup Poll](/wiki/Gallup_Poll "Gallup Poll") in November 2013 showed 61% believed in a conspiracy, and only 30% thought that Oswald did it alone.[[398]](#cite_note-400) In 1979, the [U.S. House Select Committee on Assassinations](/wiki/U.S._House_Select_Committee_on_Assassinations "U.S. House Select Committee on Assassinations") concluded, with one third of the committee dissenting, "that Kennedy was probably assassinated as a result of a [conspiracy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_assassination_conspiracy_theories "John F. Kennedy assassination conspiracy theories")." The committee was unable to identify the other gunmen or the extent of the conspiracy. This conclusion was based largely on audio recordings of the shooting.[[399]](#cite_note-401) Subsequently, investigative reports from the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") and a specially appointed [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") Committee determined that "reliable acoustic data do not support a conclusion that there was a second gunman."[[400]](#cite_note-402) The [Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") later concluded "that no persuasive evidence can be identified to support the theory of a conspiracy".[[401]](#cite_note-403)

### Funeral

Main articles: [State funeral of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/State_funeral_of_John_F._Kennedy "State funeral of John F. Kennedy") and [List of dignitaries at the state funeral of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/List_of_dignitaries_at_the_state_funeral_of_John_F._Kennedy "List of dignitaries at the state funeral of John F. Kennedy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/JFK%27s_family_leaves_Capitol_after_his_funeral%2C_1963.jpg/250px-JFK%27s_family_leaves_Capitol_after_his_funeral%2C_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK%27s_family_leaves_Capitol_after_his_funeral,_1963.jpg)

Kennedy's family leaving his [funeral](/wiki/State_funeral_of_John_F._Kennedy "State funeral of John F. Kennedy") at the [U.S. Capitol Building](/wiki/U.S._Capitol_Building "U.S. Capitol Building")

Kennedy's body was brought back to Washington. On November 23, six military pallbearers carried the flag-draped coffin into the [East Room of the White House](/wiki/East_Room "East Room"), where he lay in repose for 24 hours.[[402]](#cite_note-404)[[403]](#cite_note-405) The coffin was then transported on a horse-drawn [caisson](/wiki/Limbers_and_caissons "Limbers and caissons") to the Capitol to lie in state. Throughout the day and night, hundreds of thousands of people lined up to view the guarded casket,[[404]](#cite_note-WhitePage16-406)[[405]](#cite_note-407) with a quarter million passing through the [rotunda](/wiki/United_States_Capitol_rotunda "United States Capitol rotunda") during the 18 hours of lying in state.[[404]](#cite_note-WhitePage16-406)

Kennedy's funeral service was held on November 25, at [Cathedral of St. Matthew the Apostle](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Matthew_the_Apostle "Cathedral of St. Matthew the Apostle") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[406]](#cite_note-WhitePage17-408) The [Requiem Mass](/wiki/Requiem "Requiem") was led by [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") [Richard Cushing](/wiki/Richard_Cushing "Richard Cushing"), then the [Archbishop of Boston](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Boston "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Boston").[[406]](#cite_note-WhitePage17-408) It was attended by approximately 1,200 guests, including representatives from over 90 countries.[[407]](#cite_note-409)[[408]](#cite_note-410) After the service, Kennedy was buried at [Arlington National Cemetery](/wiki/Arlington_National_Cemetery "Arlington National Cemetery") in [Arlington County, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia").[[409]](#cite_note-411)

## Personal life, family, and reputation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/President_Kennedy_and_his_brothers._Attorney_General_Robert_F._Kennedy%2C_Senator_Edward_Moore_Kennedy%2C_President_John..._-_NARA_-_194238_-_Restoration.jpg/250px-President_Kennedy_and_his_brothers._Attorney_General_Robert_F._Kennedy%2C_Senator_Edward_Moore_Kennedy%2C_President_John..._-_NARA_-_194238_-_Restoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Kennedy_and_his_brothers._Attorney_General_Robert_F._Kennedy,_Senator_Edward_Moore_Kennedy,_President_John..._-_NARA_-_194238_-_Restoration.jpg)

The Kennedy brothers: Attorney General [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), Senator [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy"), and President John F. Kennedy at the White House in 1963

Further information: [Kennedy family](/wiki/Kennedy_family "Kennedy family")

The Kennedy family is one of the most established [political families](/wiki/Political_families "Political families") in the United States, having produced a president, three senators, three ambassadors, and numerous other representatives and public officials. While a congressman, Kennedy embarked on a seven-week trip to India, Japan, Vietnam, and Israel in 1951, at which point he became close with his then 25-year-old brother Robert, as well as his 27-year-old sister Patricia. Because they were several years apart in age, the brothers had previously seen little of each other. This 25,000-mile (40,000 km) journey was the first extended time they had spent together and resulted in their becoming best friends.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199329-412) Robert would eventually serve as his brother's [attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") and closest presidential advisor;[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199329-412) he would later run for president in [1968](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_1968_presidential_campaign "Robert F. Kennedy 1968 presidential campaign") before his [assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Robert_F._Kennedy "Assassination of Robert F. Kennedy"), while another Kennedy brother, Ted, ran for president in [1980](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy_1980_presidential_campaign "Ted Kennedy 1980 presidential campaign").[[411]](#cite_note-413)

### Wife and children

Kennedy met his wife, [Jacqueline Lee "Jackie" Bouvier](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis"), when he was a congressman. [Charles L. Bartlett](/wiki/Charles_L._Bartlett_(journalist) "Charles L. Bartlett (journalist)"), a journalist, introduced the pair at a dinner party.[[412]](#cite_note-414) They were married on September 12, 1953, at [St. Mary's Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church_Complex_(Newport,_Rhode_Island) "St. Mary's Church Complex (Newport, Rhode Island)") in [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island").[[413]](#cite_note-415)[[414]](#cite_note-416) The newlyweds honeymooned in Mexico before settling in their new home, [Hickory Hill](/wiki/Hickory_Hill_(McLean,_Virginia) "Hickory Hill (McLean, Virginia)"), in [McLean, Virginia](/wiki/McLean,_Virginia "McLean, Virginia").[[415]](#cite_note-417) In 1956, they sold their Hickory Hill estate to Kennedy's brother Robert, and bought a townhouse in [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)").[[416]](#cite_note-418)[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrinkley201236-419) The Kennedys also resided at an apartment in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), their legal residence during John's congressional career,[[418]](#cite_note-420)[[419]](#cite_note-421) and a summer home in [Cape Cod, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cape_Cod,_Massachusetts "Cape Cod, Massachusetts").[[420]](#cite_note-422)

After a miscarriage in 1955 and a stillbirth in 1956 (their daughter Arabella), their daughter [Caroline](/wiki/Caroline_Kennedy "Caroline Kennedy") was born in 1957. [John Jr.](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Jr. "John F. Kennedy Jr."), nicknamed "John-John" by the press as a child, was born in late November 1960, 17 days after his father was elected. John Jr. died in 1999 when the [small plane he was piloting crashed](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Jr._plane_crash "John F.
