Kennedy Jr. plane crash").[[421]](#cite_note-423) In August 1963, Jackie gave birth to a son, [Patrick](/wiki/Patrick_Bouvier_Kennedy "Patrick Bouvier Kennedy"); however, he died after two days due to complications from birth.[[422]](#cite_note-424)

### Popular image

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/JFK_and_family_in_Hyannis_Port%2C_04_August_1962.jpg/250px-JFK_and_family_in_Hyannis_Port%2C_04_August_1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_and_family_in_Hyannis_Port,_04_August_1962.jpg)

The First Family in [Hyannis Port, Massachusetts](/wiki/Hyannis_Port,_Massachusetts "Hyannis Port, Massachusetts"), 1962

Kennedy and his wife were younger than the presidents and first ladies who preceded them, and both were popular in the [media culture](/wiki/Media_culture "Media culture") in ways more common to pop singers and movie stars than politicians, influencing fashion trends and becoming the subjects of photo spreads in popular magazines. Although Eisenhower had allowed presidential press conferences to be filmed for television, Kennedy was the first president to ask for them to be broadcast [live](/wiki/Live_television "Live television") and made good use of the medium.[[423]](#cite_note-american_chronicle-425) In 1961, the [Radio-Television News Directors Association](/wiki/Radio_Television_Digital_News_Association "Radio Television Digital News Association") presented Kennedy with its highest honor, the [Paul White Award](/wiki/Paul_White_Award "Paul White Award"), in recognition of his open relationship with the media.[[424]](#cite_note-426)

The Kennedys invited a range of artists, writers, and intellectuals to White House dinners, raising the profile of the arts in America. On the White House lawn, they established a swimming pool and tree house, while Caroline attended a preschool with 10 other children inside the home.[[425]](#cite_note-427)[[426]](#cite_note-428)

[Vaughn Meader](/wiki/Vaughn_Meader "Vaughn Meader")'s *First Family* comedy album, which parodied the president, the first lady, their family, and the administration, sold about four million copies.[[427]](#cite_note-429)

### Health

Kennedy was plagued by childhood diseases, including [whooping cough](/wiki/Whooping_cough "Whooping cough"), [chickenpox](/wiki/Chickenpox "Chickenpox"), [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles"), and ear infections. These ailments compelled him to spend a considerable amount of time [convalescing](/wiki/Convalescing "Convalescing"). Three months prior to his third birthday, in 1920, Kennedy came down with [scarlet fever](/wiki/Scarlet_fever "Scarlet fever"), a highly contagious and life-threatening disease, and was admitted to [Boston City Hospital](/wiki/Boston_City_Hospital "Boston City Hospital").[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek200332-430)[[14]](#cite_note-jfklibrary.org-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/John_F._Kennedy_after_spinal_surgery_cph.3c33052.jpg/250px-John_F._Kennedy_after_spinal_surgery_cph.3c33052.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_F._Kennedy_after_spinal_surgery_cph.3c33052.jpg)

Kennedy and Jackie leaving the hospital following his spinal surgery, December 1954

During his years at Choate, Kennedy was beset by health problems that culminated with his emergency hospitalization in 1934 at [Yale New Haven Hospital](/wiki/Yale_New_Haven_Hospital "Yale New Haven Hospital"), where doctors suspected [leukemia](/wiki/Leukemia "Leukemia").[[429]](#cite_note-Dallek-431) While ill, he became a passionate reader and also a [fatalist](/wiki/Fatalist "Fatalist").[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews201115-432) In June 1934, he was admitted to the [Mayo Clinic](/wiki/Mayo_Clinic "Mayo Clinic") in Minnesota; the ultimate diagnosis was [colitis](/wiki/Colitis "Colitis").[[429]](#cite_note-Dallek-431) After withdrawing from Princeton University, Kennedy was hospitalized for observation at [Peter Bent Brigham Hospital](/wiki/Brigham_and_Women%27s_Hospital "Brigham and Women's Hospital") in Boston. He then spent the spring of 1936 working as a ranch hand outside [Benson, Arizona](/wiki/Benson,_Arizona "Benson, Arizona") under [Jack Speiden](/wiki/Jack_Speiden "Jack Speiden").[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek200342-433)

Years after Kennedy's death, it was revealed that in September 1947—at age 30 and during his first term in Congress—he was diagnosed by Sir Daniel Davis at [The London Clinic](/wiki/The_London_Clinic "The London Clinic") with [Addison's disease](/wiki/Addison%27s_disease "Addison's disease"). Davis estimated that Kennedy would not live for another year, while Kennedy hoped he could live for ten.[[432]](#cite_note-434) In 1966, White House physician [Janet Travell](/wiki/Janet_Travell "Janet Travell") disclosed that Kennedy also had [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"). The presence of two [endocrine diseases](/wiki/Endocrine_disease "Endocrine disease") raises the possibility that Kennedy had [autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 2](/wiki/Autoimmune_polyendocrine_syndrome_type_2 "Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 2").[[433]](#cite_note-Mandel,_Lee_R._2009_350–354-435)

Kennedy suffered from chronic severe back pain, for which he underwent surgery. His condition may have had diplomatic repercussions, as he was reportedly taking a combination of medications to manage the pain during the 1961 [Vienna Summit](/wiki/Vienna_Summit "Vienna Summit"). The combination included hormones, animal organ cells, steroids, vitamins, enzymes, and amphetamines, and possible side effects included hyperactivity, [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"), impaired judgment, nervousness, and mood swings.[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKempe2011213-436) Kennedy at one time was regularly seen by three doctors, one of whom, [Max Jacobson](/wiki/Max_Jacobson "Max Jacobson"), at first was unknown to the other two, as his mode of treatment was controversial[[435]](#cite_note-437) and used for the most severe bouts of back pain.[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves199342,_158–159-438)

Into late 1961, disagreements existed among Kennedy's doctors concerning the balance of medication and exercise. Kennedy preferred the former because he was short on time and desired immediate relief.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993243-302) The president's primary White House physician, [George G. Burkley](/wiki/George_G._Burkley "George G. Burkley"), set up gym equipment in the White House basement, where Kennedy performed stretching exercises three times a week.[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993244-439) Details of these and other medical issues were not publicly disclosed during Kennedy's lifetime.[[438]](#cite_note-440) Burkley realized that treatments by Jacobson and Travell, including excessive use of steroids and amphetamines, were medically inappropriate, and took action to remove Kennedy from their care.[[439]](#cite_note-jfkwhmd-441)

| External videos | |
| --- | --- |
| [video icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg) [Presentation by Robert Dallek on *An Unfinished Life: John F. Kennedy, 1917–1963*, May 20, 2003](https://www.c-span.org/video/?176034-1/an-unfinished-life-john-f-kennedy-1917-1963), [C-SPAN](/wiki/C-SPAN "C-SPAN") | |

In 2002, [Robert Dallek](/wiki/Robert_Dallek "Robert Dallek") wrote an extensive history of Kennedy's health based on a collection of Kennedy–associated papers from 1955 to 1963, including X-rays and prescription records from Travell. According to Travell's records, during his presidency, Kennedy suffered from high fevers; stomach, colon, and prostate issues; abscesses; high cholesterol; and adrenal problems. Travell kept a "Medicine Administration Record", cataloging Kennedy's medications:

> injected and ingested [corticosteroids](/wiki/Corticosteroids "Corticosteroids") for his adrenal insufficiency; procaine shots and ultrasound treatments and hot packs for his back; [Lomotil](/wiki/Lomotil "Lomotil"), [Metamucil](/wiki/Metamucil "Metamucil"), [paregoric](/wiki/Paregoric "Paregoric"), [phenobarbital](/wiki/Phenobarbital "Phenobarbital"), [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone_(medication) "Testosterone (medication)"), and trasentine to control his diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, and weight loss; penicillin and other antibiotics for his [urinary-tract infections](/wiki/Urinary-tract_infection "Urinary-tract infection") and an [abscess](/wiki/Abscess "Abscess"); and [Tuinal](/wiki/Tuinal "Tuinal") to help him sleep.[[429]](#cite_note-Dallek-431)

### Affairs and friendships

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/JFK_and_Marilyn_Monroe_1962.jpg/250px-JFK_and_Marilyn_Monroe_1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_and_Marilyn_Monroe_1962.jpg)

Attorney General [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"), and John F. Kennedy talk during the president's May 19, 1962, early birthday party, where Monroe publicly serenaded Kennedy with "[Happy Birthday, Mr. President](/wiki/Happy_Birthday,_Mr._President "Happy Birthday, Mr. President")"

Kennedy was single in the 1940s and had relationships with Danish journalist [Inga Arvad](/wiki/Inga_Arvad "Inga Arvad")[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek200383–85-442) and actress [Gene Tierney](/wiki/Gene_Tierney "Gene Tierney").[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOsborne2006195-443) During his time as a senator, he had an affair with [Gunilla von Post](/wiki/Gunilla_von_Post "Gunilla von Post") who later wrote that the future president tried to end his marriage to be with her before he and his wife had any children.[[442]](#cite_note-444) Kennedy was also reported to have had affairs with [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"),[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993315–316-445) [Judith Campbell](/wiki/Judith_Exner "Judith Exner"),[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993289-446) [Mary Pinchot Meyer](/wiki/Mary_Pinchot_Meyer "Mary Pinchot Meyer"),[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003475-447) [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich"),[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek200358-30) White House intern [Mimi Alford](/wiki/Mimi_Alford "Mimi Alford"),[[446]](#cite_note-nytimes_garrow-448) and his wife's [press secretary](/wiki/Press_secretary "Press secretary"), [Pamela Turnure](/wiki/Pamela_Turnure "Pamela Turnure").[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003475,_476-449) Several conspiracy theories have surrounded [Monroe's death](/wiki/Death_of_Marilyn_Monroe "Death of Marilyn Monroe"), alleging that Kennedy may have been involved.[[448]](#cite_note-450)

The full extent of Kennedy's relationship with Monroe—who in 1962 famously sang "[Happy Birthday, Mr. President](/wiki/Happy_Birthday,_Mr._President "Happy Birthday, Mr. President")" at Kennedy's birthday celebration at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden_(1925) "Madison Square Garden (1925)")—is not known, though it has been reported that they spent a weekend together in March 1962 while he was staying at [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby")'s house.[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeaming2006379–380-451) Furthermore, people at the White House switchboard noted that Monroe had called Kennedy during 1962.[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003581-452) J. Edgar Hoover, the FBI director, received reports about Kennedy's indiscretions.[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003376-453) These included an alleged tryst with a suspected [East German](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") spy, [Ellen Rometsch](/wiki/Ellen_Rometsch "Ellen Rometsch"). According to historian [Michael Beschloss](/wiki/Michael_Beschloss "Michael Beschloss"), in July 1963, Hoover reportedly informed Robert Kennedy about the affair with a woman "suspected as a Soviet intelligence agent, someone linked to East German intelligence." Robert reportedly took the matter seriously enough to raise it with leading Democratic and Republican figures in Congress.[[452]](#cite_note-454)[[453]](#cite_note-455) However, the FBI never turned up "any solid evidence" that Rometsch was a spy or that she had a relationship with President Kennedy.[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2000255–256-456) Former Secret Service agent Larry Newman recalled "morale problems" that the president's indiscretions engendered within the Secret Service.[[455]](#cite_note-457)

Kennedy inspired affection and loyalty from the members of his team and his supporters.[[456]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes2007116-458) According to Reeves, this included "the logistics of Kennedy's liaisons ... [which] required secrecy and devotion rare in the annals of the energetic service demanded by successful politicians."[[457]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves1993291-459) Kennedy believed that his friendly relationship with members of the press would help protect him from public revelations about his sex life.[[458]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDallek2003478-460)

### Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/President_John_F._Kennedy_with_the_Boston_Celtics_%2801%29_%28A%29.jpg/250px-President_John_F._Kennedy_with_the_Boston_Celtics_%2801%29_%28A%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_John_F._Kennedy_with_the_Boston_Celtics_(01)_(A).jpg)

President John F. Kennedy with the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics"), January 1963

Kennedy was a fan of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") and the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics").[[459]](#cite_note-Shaping_Up_America:_JFK,_Sports_and-461)[[460]](#cite_note-462) Growing up on Cape Cod, Kennedy and his siblings developed a lifelong passion for [sailing](/wiki/Sailing "Sailing").[[461]](#cite_note-463) He also took up [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"); playing regularly at the [Hyannisport Club](/wiki/Hyannisport_Club "Hyannisport Club") in Massachusetts and the Palm Beach Country Club in Florida.[[459]](#cite_note-Shaping_Up_America:_JFK,_Sports_and-461)[[462]](#cite_note-464)

### Religion

See also: [John F. Kennedy 1960 presidential campaign § Religion](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_1960_presidential_campaign#Religion "John F. Kennedy 1960 presidential campaign")

Kennedy was the first [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") elected to the presidency.[[463]](#cite_note-465) During his childhood, he attended [St. Aidan's Church](/wiki/St._Aidan%27s_Church_(Brookline,_Massachusetts) "St. Aidan's Church (Brookline, Massachusetts)") in Brookline, Massachusetts, where he was [baptized](/wiki/Baptized "Baptized") on June 19, 1917.[[464]](#cite_note-466) From 1946 until he died in 1963, he served on the advisory board of the Roman Catholic [Emmanuel College](/wiki/Emmanuel_College_(Massachusetts) "Emmanuel College (Massachusetts)").[[465]](#cite_note-auto4-467)

## Historical evaluations and legacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/JFK_library_Stitch_Crop.jpg/330px-JFK_library_Stitch_Crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_library_Stitch_Crop.jpg)

The [John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")

### Presidency

Further information: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy § Historical reputation](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy#Historical_reputation "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

Historians and political scientists tend to [rank](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#Scholar_survey_summary "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") Kennedy as an above-average president, and he is usually the highest-ranking president who served less than one full term.[[466]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiglio2006308–309-468) A 2010 survey by the [Gallup Organization](/wiki/Gallup_Organization "Gallup Organization"), when Americans were asked their opinions of modern presidents, Kennedy was found to be the most-popular, with an 85 percent retrospective approval rating.[[467]](#cite_note-469) A 2014 survey from *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* of 162 members of the [American Political Science Association](/wiki/American_Political_Science_Association "American Political Science Association")'s Presidents and Executive Politics section ranked Kennedy 14th highest overall among the 43 persons who have been president, including then-president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"). The survey found Kennedy to be the most overrated U.S. president.[[468]](#cite_note-470) A 2017 [C-SPAN](/wiki/C-SPAN "C-SPAN") survey has Kennedy ranked among the top ten presidents.[[469]](#cite_note-471) A 2023 Gallup survey showed Kennedy with a retrospective approval rating of 90 percent, the highest of all U.S. presidents in recent history.[[470]](#cite_note-472) Assessments of his policies are mixed.[[471]](#cite_note-JFK-IL-473)[[472]](#cite_note-abrinkley1-474) Many of Kennedy's legislative proposals were passed after his death, during the [Johnson administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson"), and Kennedy's death gave those proposals a powerful moral component.[[473]](#cite_note-tgillman-475)

Kennedy came in third (behind Martin Luther King Jr. and [Mother Teresa](/wiki/Mother_Teresa "Mother Teresa")) in [Gallup's List of Widely Admired People](/wiki/Gallup%27s_List_of_Widely_Admired_People "Gallup's List of Widely Admired People") of the 20th century.[[474]](#cite_note-476)[[475]](#cite_note-477) In 1961, he was awarded the [Laetare Medal](/wiki/Laetare_Medal "Laetare Medal") by the [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame"), considered the most prestigious award for [American Catholics](/wiki/American_Catholics "American Catholics").[[476]](#cite_note-478) He was posthumously awarded the [*Pacem in Terris* Award](/wiki/Pacem_in_Terris_Award "Pacem in Terris Award") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"): Peace on Earth) and the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom").[[477]](#cite_note-479)

#### Camelot

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/John_F_Kennedy_Official_Portrait.jpg/250px-John_F_Kennedy_Official_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_F_Kennedy_Official_Portrait.jpg)

Official White House portrait of Kennedy, by [Aaron Shikler](/wiki/Aaron_Shikler "Aaron Shikler")

The term "Camelot" is often used to describe his presidency, reflecting both the mythic grandeur accorded Kennedy in death and powerful nostalgia for that era of American history.[[478]](#cite_note-480) According to Richard Dean Burns and Joseph M. Siracusa, the most popular theme surrounding Kennedy's legacy is its replay of the legend of [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur") and [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot "Camelot") from [Arthurian Literature](/wiki/Arthurian_Literature "Arthurian Literature").[[479]](#cite_note-481) In an interview following Kennedy's death, his widow Jacqueline mentioned his affection for the Broadway musical *[Camelot](/wiki/Camelot_(musical) "Camelot (musical)")* and quoted its closing lines: "Don't let it be forgot, that once there was a spot, for one brief, shining moment that was known as Camelot."[[480]](#cite_note-JFKlibrary.org_White._Series_11._Camelot_Documents-482)[[481]](#cite_note-483) Critics, especially historians, have dismissed the Camelot myth as a distortion of Kennedy's actions, beliefs, and policies. However, in the public memory, the years of Kennedy's presidency are still seen as a brief, brilliant, and shining moment.[[482]](#cite_note-484)[[483]](#cite_note-485)

### Memorials and eponyms

Main article: [List of things named after John F. Kennedy](/wiki/List_of_things_named_after_John_F._Kennedy "List of things named after John F. Kennedy")

Examples of the extensive list include:

* [John F. Kennedy Space Center](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Space_Center "John F. Kennedy Space Center") (formerly NASA Launch Operations Center), [Merritt Island, Florida](/wiki/Merritt_Island,_Florida "Merritt Island, Florida") – renamed on November 29, 1963
* [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") (formerly Idlewild Airport), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), New York City – renamed on December 24, 1963
* [USS *John F. Kennedy* (CV-67)](/wiki/USS_John_F._Kennedy_(CV-67) "USS John F. Kennedy (CV-67)") – U.S. Navy aircraft carrier ordered in April 1964, launched in May 1967, and decommissioned in August 2007;
* [Kennedy half dollar](/wiki/Kennedy_half_dollar "Kennedy half dollar") – first minted in 1964
* [John F. Kennedy School of Government](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_School_of_Government "John F. Kennedy School of Government"), part of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") – renamed in 1966
* [John F. Kennedy Federal Building](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Federal_Building "John F. Kennedy Federal Building"), [Government Center](/wiki/Government_Center,_Boston "Government Center, Boston") section of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), – opened in 1966
* [John Fitzgerald Kennedy Memorial](/wiki/John_Fitzgerald_Kennedy_Memorial "John Fitzgerald Kennedy Memorial"), [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") – opened in 1970
* [John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") – named in 1964; opened in 1971
* [John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum"), [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Boston "Dorchester, Boston") section of Boston, adjacent to the [University of Massachusetts Boston](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Boston "University of Massachusetts Boston") – opened in 1979
* [Statue of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Statue_of_John_F._Kennedy_(Boston) "Statue of John F. Kennedy (Boston)") by Isabel McIlvain, on the grounds of the [Massachusetts State House](/wiki/Massachusetts_State_House "Massachusetts State House"), Boston – dedicated on May 29, 1990
